title: Render purity and Strict Mode

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific React: Component-Based Browser Applications draft for Render purity and Strict Mode; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: React Learn and Reference; React 19.2; provisions applicable to Render purity and Strict Mode
sources.claim: Pure render returns the same description for the same inputs and avoids mutation, subscriptions, network calls, and DOM writes.

sources.locator: React Versions; 19.2.7, June 2026; provisions applicable to Render purity and Strict Mode
sources.claim: Pure render returns the same description for the same inputs and avoids mutation, subscriptions, network calls, and DOM writes.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Pure render returns the same description for the same inputs and avoids mutation, subscriptions, network calls, and DOM writes.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A component computes filtered items from props during render without pushing into the original array. Your goal is to find and remove a render-time mutation revealed by Strict Mode. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Components and JSX; Props and one-way data flow; javascript scope closures. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Pure render returns the same description for the same inputs and avoids mutation, subscriptions, network calls, and DOM writes. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Time, randomness, module mutation, and id generation can make output unstable unless managed through supported mechanisms. This draft therefore rejects: A fetch call during render runs once because React renders components only once.

includes: Avoid side effects during render and use development double invocation to expose impurities.
Find and remove a render-time mutation revealed by Strict Mode.
excludes: Time, randomness, module mutation, and id generation can make output unstable unless managed through supported mechanisms.

outcomes: Find and remove a render-time mutation revealed by Strict Mode.

examples.title: Render purity and Strict Mode: concrete case
examples.situation: A component computes filtered items from props during render without pushing into the original array.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A component computes filtered items from props during render without pushing into the original array.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Render can repeat or be discarded; keep side effects out of render and synchronize external systems in events or effects.
examples.result: The result follows because Render can repeat or be discarded; keep side effects out of render and synchronize external systems in events or effects.

examples.title: Render purity and Strict Mode: boundary case
examples.situation: Time, randomness, module mutation, and id generation can make output unstable unless managed through supported mechanisms.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Time, randomness, module mutation, and id generation can make output unstable unless managed through supported mechanisms.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: A fetch call during render runs once because React renders components only once.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Render can repeat or be discarded; keep side effects out of render and synchronize external systems in events or effects.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete React: Component-Based Browser Applications artifact to explain why this belief fails: A fetch call during render runs once because React renders components only once.

limitations: A workshop does not reproduce reconciliation identity, hook ordering, concurrent rendering, effects, or browser accessibility APIs.

recap.summary: You should now be able to find and remove a render-time mutation revealed by Strict Mode. Keep this boundary in view: Time, randomness, module mutation, and id generation can make output unstable unless managed through supported mechanisms.
